title: ER Diagrams and Database Design
summary: Learn how Entity-Relationship (ER) diagrams model real-world data, and how to design multi-table databases with correct relationships.
content_html:
Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagrams

An ER diagram is a visual model of the data in a system before creating any tables. It shows entities (things we store data about), their attributes, and the relationships between them. ER diagrams are the blueprint used to design database tables.

Entity     = a thing about which data is stored (e.g. Movie, Actor, Rating)
Attribute  = a property of an entity (e.g. title, runtime, ratingCode)
Relationship = how two entities are connected (e.g. Actor acts in Movie)

Relationship Types

One-to-One  (1:1)  - one row in A matches at most one row in B
                     Example: one person has one passport

One-to-Many (1:N)  - one row in A matches many rows in B
                     Example: one rating code applies to many movies

Many-to-Many (M:N) - many rows in A match many rows in B
                     Example: one actor appears in many movies;
                               one movie has many actors
                     Resolved by an INTERSECTION (junction) table

Resolving Many-to-Many Relationships

A Many-to-Many relationship between Actor and Movie cannot be stored directly in a relational database. We create a Casting table to resolve it:

Actor table:    actorNo | firstName | surname | gender
Movie table:    movieNo | title | relyear | runtime | ratingCode
Casting table:  castId (PK) | actorNo (FK) | movieNo (FK)

Each row in Casting represents ONE actor in ONE movie:
castId | actorNo | movieNo
1      | 51329   | 82693    <- Bradley Cooper in Silver Linings
2      | 72129   | 82693    <- Jennifer Lawrence in Silver Linings
3      | 72129   | 70160    <- Jennifer Lawrence in Hunger Games

Relationships Diagram (Updated Movie Database)

Rating (PK: ratingCode)
    |
    | 1:N
    |
Movie (PK: movieNo, FK: ratingCode, FK: colour_code)
    |                    |
    | 1:N                | 1:N
    |                    |
Casting (PK: castId)  ColourType (PK: colour_code)
    |
    | N:1
    |
Actor (PK: actorNo)

Normalisation Principles

Good database design follows normalisation rules to eliminate redundancy and update anomalies. The key principle: store each fact in exactly one place. If rating descriptions are stored in the Movie table and a description changes, you must update every row — this is an update anomaly. Separate tables avoid this.

1NF: Atomic values — no repeating groups or multi-valued fields
2NF: No partial dependencies on a composite key
3NF: No transitive dependencies (non-key fields depend only on the PK)

Indexes and Performance

An index is a sorted data structure the RDBMS maintains alongside a table to speed up searches. The RDBMS automatically creates a Primary Key index. Binary search on a 100,000-row index takes ~17 comparisons instead of 50,000 sequential reads. Trade-off: indexes speed up reads but slow down writes (every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must also update each index).

exercises.title: ER diagram purpose
exercises.prompt: What is the purpose of an ER diagram?
options: To visually model entities and their relationships before creating tables
To display data to end users
exercises.answer: To visually model entities and their relationships before creating tables

exercises.title: Many-to-many resolution
exercises.prompt: How is a Many-to-Many relationship resolved in a relational database?
options: By creating an intersection (junction) table with two foreign keys
By duplicating data in both tables
By using a single column with comma-separated values
By choosing one side to be Many-to-One
exercises.answer: By creating an intersection (junction) table with two foreign keys

exercises.title: Actor-Movie relationship
exercises.prompt: What type of relationship exists between Actor and Movie?

exercises.title: 1NF
options: All column values are atomic (no repeating groups)
No column depends on another non-key column
All tables have a primary key
exercises.answer: All column values are atomic (no repeating groups)

exercises.title: Index trade-off
exercises.prompt: What is the main trade-off of creating many indexes on a table?
options: Faster reads but slower writes, since each index must be updated on every change
More disk space but faster writes
exercises.answer: Faster reads but slower writes, since each index must be updated on every change

exercises.title: Update anomaly
options: The same fact is stored in multiple places and must be updated in each
The database has too many tables
exercises.answer: The same fact is stored in multiple places and must be updated in each

exercises.title: Casting table
exercises.prompt: In the movie database, the Casting table contains which columns?

exercises.title: 3NF
options: Transitive dependencies — non-key columns that depend on another non-key column
exercises.answer: Transitive dependencies — non-key columns that depend on another non-key column
